title: A Different Shade of Gray: Midlife and Beyond in the Inner City
abstract: A Different Shade of Gray: Midlife and Beyond in the Inner City. Katherine S. Newman. New York: The New Press. 2003. 306 pp. ISBN 1-56584-615-X. $29.95 (cloth). Katherine Newman's book examines a long-neglected group within the inner city: minorities who are middle aged and older. African American, Puerto Rican, and Dominican respondents in New York City participated in life history interviews. Newman discusses these data against a backdrop of both a larger New York city survey and national data derived from middle-aged and older persons. Her resultant ability to compare her respondents to their neighbors and to minorities and Whites nationally strengthens her conclusions about her respondents’ situations and how they might be ameliorated. These older inner-city residents, who have worked hard against many odds, are beset by health, income, family, and safety problems that are unthinkable for most middle-class Americans, and contradictory to dominant conceptions concerning how middle age and retirement should unfold. Their stories convey a sense of strength and resilience, but Newman makes it clear that, without policy intervention, they will often struggle in vain. Her book is informed by a life course approach and consequent attention to both local history—such as neighborhood changes, work cycles in response to economic changes, and the crack wars—and the related national landscape. Together, these help explain how their lives have been shaped by both opportunities, such as economic upswings and the public sector jobs opened by affirmative action, and constraints, such as the intersecting class, race, and gender discrimination in labor markets that single mothers face. The most engagingly written chapters, such as Chapters 1 (which provides an overview of the book) and 3, begin with the stories of different persons who serve as exemplars to flesh out the main points. Chapter 2 provides what Newman refers to as “the big picture”: a statistical comparison of Whites and minorities in relation to childhood poverty, education, employment, income, marriage patterns, and health. Chapters 3 through 5 look at different dimensions that shape the aging experiences of her inner-city residents: work and neighborhood locations, men's and women's relationships in middle and later years, and the effects of economic hardship on physical and mental health. The emerging picture is that work and family/kin obligations intersect for both men and women to influence their later lives through such things as health, family structure, and pension availability. Chapter 6, the longest and perhaps most interesting chapter, explores racial and ethnic relations among different groups in inner-city New York. Readers must bear in mind, however, that the chapter is about perceptions and is not necessarily meant to assert, for instance, whether one group really has more of a “work ethic” than another. One significant finding is that each group understands that minority group membership provides structural barriers, but they also hold individuals accountable for their choices. The dominant cultural belief in individualism and personal responsibility is upheld; minority group members may have more obstacles to overcome, but it is up to them to do so. Further, even if individuals adopt stereotypical beliefs about other racial and ethic groups, they tend to be most critical of their own communities. Chapter 7 demonstrates that, though individuals may not participate in typical middle-class volunteer activities, they see their obligations to their families as part of their public duty. Raising one's children “right” is a civic duty that helps create peace, rather than chaos, in the area. Caring for elderly or other relatives relieves the wider society of that obligation. Neighborhood responsibilities include organized activities (like escort services) and a norm of reciprocity that mandates helping others. At the level of the larger racial and ethnic community, group members feel responsible to take care of their own. The final chapter contains Newman's policy recommendations for improving the lives of these inner-city residents. Not only does she indicate the deleterious effects that Social Security reform would have but she also advocates correcting the biases in the present system that serve to disadvantage women and minorities. She argues for policies that would improve overall health and health care access; that would help families caring for increased numbers of kin; and that would protect inner-city residents from crime. As much as I liked this book, some drawbacks lead me to recommend caution in using it in undergraduate classes. First, mixing those in their 40s with those in their 60s or 70s throws together those who may be of different generations and at varying stages of the life cycle. For example, middle-aged women with children in the home are not the same as grandmothers raising grandchildren despite the similarity in childrearing situation. Further, these lives differ still from those who grow old without grandchildren and who may face some similar but also divergent sets of issues. Generalizing across such different groups is problematic. Second, Newman sometimes presents her respondents’ views without analysis. For instance, we read that Sheila blames many of African Americans’ problems on the prevalence of single-mother households; fathers and positive male role models are critical (p. 171). Newman lists the indicators that lead Sheila to believe that her community is falling apart, but does not comment further, thereby allowing Sheila's views to take on an aura of reality. Still, these problems are minor and do not detract from the book's overall value to scholars with an interest in families, aging, racial and ethnic relations, poverty, and urban life, among others. The complex and nuanced portrayal makes it a superb choice for graduate instruction and perhaps a good one for upper-level undergraduates.
